exam.title: Computer Networking — Practice Exam
exam.subtitle: Foundations: Switching, Delay Models, Throughput, Protocol Layering & Packet Segmentation

highest_priority_topics: Circuit vs. packet switching: resource allocation philosophy, statistical multiplexing, when each is better
The four delay components: precise formulas for transmission, propagation, queuing, and processing
Transmission vs. propagation delay: the caravan analogy and why students routinely confuse them
Throughput and bottleneck link: min(Rs, Rc, R/N) in shared backbone scenarios
Store-and-forward semantics: entire packet must arrive before forwarding begins, and what this costs
Segmentation and pipelining delay formula: (N + P - 1) × (L/R) and the intuition behind it
Statistical multiplexing math: binomial probability of congestion with n users, probability p active
Protocol layering: encapsulation/decapsulation, PDU vs. SDU, peer-to-peer vs. service interface
The hourglass model and IP as the narrow waist: what it enables and what it constrains
End-to-end principle: why complexity lives at the edges, what the dumb core must not do
Routing vs. forwarding: global path computation vs. local table lookup — the distinction matters
FDM vs. TDM: how each divides resources and the idle-waste problem in circuit switching
common_misconceptions_targeted: Confusing transmission delay (time to push bits onto link) with propagation delay (time for bits to travel the link)
Thinking packet switching is always better than circuit switching — real-time constant-rate traffic favors circuits
Assuming store-and-forward doubles delay for every hop — students forget pipelining overlaps hop transmissions
Misapplying the bottleneck formula by ignoring the shared backbone R/N term when N connections share a link
Thinking statistical multiplexing guarantees no congestion — it only makes congestion extremely unlikely
Conflating routing (global algorithm) with forwarding (local lookup) — treating them as synonyms
Believing the PDU passed between peers is identical to the SDU received from above — the header addition is the whole point
Assuming the hourglass means IP is the best possible narrow waist — it is a historical lock-in, not an optimal choice
Thinking segmentation always reduces total delay — the formula shows it reduces delay but adds header overhead
Confusing FDM (always-on narrow band) with TDM (full-rate periodic slot) — students blend them together

sections.title: True / False
sections.instructions: State TRUE or FALSE, then write 2–3 sentences justifying your answer. No credit for the answer alone.

questions.prompt: A network engineer doubles the bandwidth of a fiber link from 1 Gbps to 2 Gbps. This change will cut in half the time it takes for the first bit of a packet to arrive at the far end of the link.

answer.explanation: Doubling the link bandwidth halves the transmission delay — the time to push all bits of a packet onto the link (d_trans = L/R). However, the time for the first bit to arrive at the far end is dominated by propagation delay (d_prop = d/s), which depends solely on the physical length of the link and the speed of light in the medium. Doubling the bandwidth does not change how fast bits travel through the fiber — that is a physical property of the medium. The first bit still takes exactly the same time to propagate across the link regardless of the link rate.
answer.common_mistake: Students conflate bandwidth (a rate — how fast bits are pushed onto the link) with propagation speed (how fast a bit travels through the medium). Increasing one has zero effect on the other.
answer.deeper_concept: The fundamental independence of transmission delay and propagation delay; why high-bandwidth satellite links still have high latency (propagation delay dominates across 36,000 km)

questions.prompt: In a circuit-switched network, once a call is established, the reserved bandwidth is available to other users if the original caller is temporarily silent (not sending data).

answer.explanation: Circuit switching dedicates resources exclusively to a connection for its entire duration, regardless of whether those resources are being used. If a caller falls silent, their allocated frequency band (FDM) or time slot (TDM) sits idle — other users cannot reclaim it. This is the defining inefficiency of circuit switching: reserved resources are wasted during idle periods. This is precisely why packet switching's statistical multiplexing is more efficient for bursty traffic — resources are only consumed when data is actually being sent.
answer.common_mistake: Students confuse circuit switching with something like a reservation system where unused slots can be reclaimed. The entire point of the reservation is exclusivity — giving it back would break the performance guarantees the circuit was designed to provide.
answer.deeper_concept: The fundamental resource allocation tradeoff: guaranteed exclusivity (circuit) vs. shared efficiency (packet); why statistical multiplexing outperforms static allocation for bursty traffic

questions.prompt: In a store-and-forward network, sending a 3-packet message across 4 links takes exactly 4 times as long as sending it across 1 link, because each router must receive all 3 packets before forwarding any of them.

answer.explanation: Store-and-forward requires a router to receive an entire individual packet before forwarding it — but it does NOT require a router to receive all packets of a message before forwarding the first one. Pipelining allows Router 1 to forward Packet 1 to Router 2 while the source is still transmitting Packet 2 to Router 1. For P packets over N links, total time is (N + P - 1) × (L/R), not N × P × (L/R). For 3 packets over 4 links, that is (4 + 3 - 1) = 6 packet-transmission-times, not 12. The claim in the prompt describes a much worse scenario that does not exist.
answer.common_mistake: Students apply store-and-forward at the message level (all packets) rather than the packet level (each individual packet). This leads to wildly pessimistic delay estimates and a misunderstanding of why segmentation helps.
answer.deeper_concept: The distinction between store-and-forward semantics (per-packet) and the pipeline parallelism it still enables across multiple hops; why (N + P - 1) not N × P

questions.prompt: Statistical multiplexing in packet switching guarantees that congestion will never occur as long as the average traffic load is below the link capacity.

answer.explanation: Statistical multiplexing makes congestion unlikely — not impossible. Even when average load is well below capacity, instantaneous bursts can cause multiple users to become active simultaneously, temporarily exceeding capacity and causing queuing or packet loss. The binomial probability model shows that for any finite number of users with nonzero activity probability p, there is always some probability that more than the capacity-equivalent number of users are simultaneously active. Statistical multiplexing is an efficiency bet on the burstiness of traffic, not a guarantee. That is why routers have buffers and why packet loss is a normal part of the Internet's operation.
answer.common_mistake: Students read 'statistical multiplexing is more efficient' and infer 'therefore congestion-free.' The qualifier 'statistical' itself signals that the outcome is probabilistic, not deterministic.
answer.deeper_concept: The probabilistic nature of packet-switched network performance; why best-effort delivery and drop-on-overflow are fundamental design features, not flaws

questions.prompt: When the Transport layer on the sender passes a segment to the Network layer, both layers are exchanging the same Protocol Data Unit (PDU) — the segment. The Network layer simply forwards it unchanged.

answer.explanation: The Transport layer's PDU (a segment) becomes the Network layer's SDU — the payload that is handed down as a service data unit. The Network layer then encapsulates this SDU by prepending its own header (IP header), creating a new PDU called a datagram. The segment is not forwarded unchanged; it is wrapped. At the receiver, the process reverses: the Network layer strips its header and passes the original segment up as an SDU to the Transport layer. The key insight is that each layer's PDU = its SDU + its own header, creating the nested 'Matryoshka doll' structure of encapsulation.
answer.common_mistake: Students conflate the SDU (what is received from above) with the PDU (what is exchanged with the peer layer). The addition of the header is the entire mechanism by which a layer adds its own addressing and control information.
answer.deeper_concept: Encapsulation as the core mechanism of layered networking; how each layer's header carries the information needed by its peer at the receiver to perform its function

sections.title: Delay and Throughput Calculations
sections.instructions: Show all work. State which delay component(s) you are computing at each step. A correct answer with no supporting work receives no credit.

scenario.setup: A router connects two links. The incoming link has bandwidth 500 Mbps. The outgoing link has bandwidth 100 Mbps and a physical length of 4000 km. The propagation speed in the medium is 2 × 10^8 m/s. A 12,000-bit packet arrives at the router. There are currently 5 other packets of the same size already queued at the output port. Processing delay at the router is 2 microseconds.
scenario.task: Compute the total nodal delay experienced by this packet at this router. Break down each of the four components with units.

queuing_delay.note: The packet must wait for the 5 queued packets to finish transmitting before it can begin. The incoming link speed is irrelevant here — queuing delay is driven by the outgoing link rate.

transmission_delay.note: Time to push this packet's bits onto the outgoing 100 Mbps link. The incoming 500 Mbps rate does not affect this.

propagation_delay.note: Time for the last bit to travel to the next router 4000 km away. Dominates the total in this scenario.

total.dominant_component: Propagation delay (20 ms) dominates because of the 4000 km link length.

answer.common_mistake: Students use the incoming link rate (500 Mbps) for transmission delay instead of the outgoing rate (100 Mbps). The packet exits on the outgoing link — that rate determines how long it takes to transmit. Also, students often forget to compute queuing delay as the service time for all previously queued packets, not just a generic wait time.
answer.deeper_concept: Why long-haul links are dominated by propagation delay, not transmission delay; why adding bandwidth to a transoceanic cable does not reduce latency; the role of each delay component in different network scenarios

scenario.setup: A source host wants to send a 7.2 megabit (7,200,000-bit) file to a destination host through 3 intermediate routers (4 links total). Each link has a bandwidth of 1.2 Mbps. Ignore propagation and processing delays.

parts.label: A
parts.prompt: How long does it take to deliver the file if it is sent as a single, unsegmented packet?

parts.label: B
parts.prompt: How long does it take if the file is segmented into 1200 packets of 6000 bits each?

parts.label: C
parts.prompt: Why is Part B faster? Explain the mechanism in one or two sentences. Then identify one cost that Part B incurs that Part A does not.

parts.label: D
parts.prompt: A network engineer proposes making packets smaller — say 600 bits each (yielding 12,000 packets). Without calculating the exact answer, explain whether this will be faster, slower, or the same as Part B, and why.

A.note: Each router must receive the entire 7.2 Mb file before forwarding. No parallelism.

C.mechanism: Segmentation enables pipelining: while Router 1 forwards Packet 1 to Router 2, the source is simultaneously sending Packet 2 to Router 1, and so on. All links in the path are kept busy in parallel rather than sequentially. The end-to-end time collapses from N × (M/R) to approximately M/R + (N-1) × (L/R), where the second term is the small pipeline startup overhead.
C.cost: Every packet requires its own header. With 1200 packets, 1200 headers must be created, transmitted, and parsed. This adds bandwidth overhead (header bits / total bits) and increases per-packet processing load at every router compared to the single large packet.

D.answer: Making packets smaller to 600 bits yields 12,000 packets. The (N + P - 1) term becomes (4 + 12,000 - 1) = 12,003 vs. 1,203 for 1200 packets. T = 12,003 × (600/1,200,000) = 12,003 × 0.0005 = 6.0015 s. This is marginally faster in the formula, but in practice the result would be slower or equal due to the dramatically increased header-to-payload ratio (12,000 headers vs. 1,200) and the router processing overhead of handling 10× more packets. The delay formula ignores header overhead; in a real system, very small packets are less efficient.
D.key_insight: The formula shows diminishing returns as P grows large — (N + P - 1) × (L/R) ≈ P × (L/R) = M/R for large P, which is constant. The formula approaches M/R + (N-1)×(L/R) asymptotically. Smaller packets don't meaningfully help delay but do meaningfully hurt overhead.

answer.common_mistake: For Part A, students apply the segmentation formula with P=1 instead of the simpler N × (M/R). They are equivalent — (N + 1 - 1) × (M/R) = N × (M/R) — but students should recognize both forms. For Part B, students drop the '-1' from (N + P - 1), which is a small error here but matters conceptually.
answer.deeper_concept: Why pipelining reduces delay by converting a sequential process into a parallel one; the tradeoff between packet size, header overhead, and pipeline efficiency

scenario.setup: Consider the following end-to-end path: A server with an outgoing access link of 80 Mbps connects through a shared backbone link of 200 Mbps to a client with an incoming access link of 30 Mbps. The backbone link is shared by 5 simultaneous connections (including this one).

parts.label: A
parts.prompt: What is the end-to-end throughput for this connection?

parts.label: B
parts.prompt: The server upgrades its access link to 160 Mbps. What is the new end-to-end throughput? Did the upgrade help?

parts.label: C
parts.prompt: What single infrastructure upgrade would most improve throughput for this connection, and by how much?

A.values: R_s = 80 Mbps, R_c = 30 Mbps, R/N = 200/5 = 40 Mbps
A.bottleneck: The client's access link at 30 Mbps is the bottleneck.

B.new_values: R_s = 160 Mbps, R_c = 30 Mbps, R/N = 40 Mbps
B.conclusion: The upgrade did not help at all. The bottleneck is the client's access link, not the server's. Upgrading a non-bottleneck link has zero effect on end-to-end throughput — a fundamental and often expensive lesson in network capacity planning.

C.answer: Upgrading the client's access link from 30 Mbps to anything above 40 Mbps would shift the bottleneck. Specifically, upgrading to 40+ Mbps makes the backbone share (40 Mbps) the new bottleneck. Upgrading the client link to 40 Mbps raises throughput from 30 Mbps to 40 Mbps — a 33% improvement. Upgrading the client link further (e.g., to 80 Mbps) doesn't help until the backbone is also upgraded or has fewer sharing connections.

answer.common_mistake: Students average the three rates instead of taking the minimum. They also commonly ignore the R/N term entirely, treating the backbone as having full 200 Mbps capacity per connection.
answer.deeper_concept: Bottleneck identification as a prerequisite for any effective capacity upgrade; why improving non-bottleneck links wastes money; the min() nature of end-to-end throughput

sections.title: Short Answer — Conceptual
sections.instructions: Answer in 3–6 sentences. Explain the 'why', not just the 'what'. Vague or definition-only answers receive no credit.

questions.topic: Circuit vs. Packet Switching — When Circuit Wins
questions.prompt: A telecommunications company is designing the network infrastructure for two separate applications: (1) a real-time remote surgery system that transmits 4K video and haptic feedback with zero tolerance for jitter or delay variation, and (2) a popular video-on-demand streaming service for millions of casual viewers. Which switching technology — circuit or packet — is more appropriate for each application, and why? Be specific about what property of each technology drives your recommendation.

application_1.reasoning: Remote surgery requires guaranteed constant bandwidth and zero queuing delay/jitter — a surgeon cannot tolerate feedback that is delayed or uneven due to network congestion. Circuit switching reserves dedicated resources for the duration of the connection, eliminating the possibility of queuing (no buffer contention with other users) and guaranteeing constant transmission timing. The cost of idle resource waste is acceptable given the life-critical performance guarantee needed.

application_2.reasoning: Video-on-demand is bursty traffic at scale — millions of users watching at different rates, pausing, buffering, or seeking. Circuit switching would require pre-allocating bandwidth for every concurrent viewer, which would be astronomically wasteful since viewers are rarely sending at their maximum rate simultaneously. Packet switching exploits statistical multiplexing: the probability that all users demand peak bandwidth simultaneously is negligibly small, so the same physical infrastructure can serve far more users than circuit switching could at the same cost. Minor buffering at the client side absorbs any jitter.

answer.key_distinction: The decision turns on traffic burstiness and tolerance for delay variation. Constant, latency-sensitive, low-tolerance applications favor circuits; bursty, delay-tolerant, large-scale applications favor packets.
answer.common_mistake: Students say 'packet switching is always better' without acknowledging that 'better' depends entirely on the application's traffic profile and performance requirements. The Internet works on packet switching not because circuits are always inferior, but because most Internet traffic is bursty.
answer.deeper_concept: The fundamental engineering tradeoff between guaranteed performance (circuit) and statistical efficiency (packet); why the Internet is packet-switched and the phone network was circuit-switched

questions.topic: Routing vs. Forwarding — The Critical Distinction
questions.prompt: A student says: 'Routing and forwarding are the same thing — both just decide where to send a packet.' Explain why this is wrong, using the specific terms control plane and data plane. Describe what would break if you collapsed both functions into a single, slow process running on the router's main CPU.

answer.correct_distinction: Forwarding is a local, per-packet, data-plane operation: when a packet arrives, the router looks up its destination IP in a pre-built forwarding table and moves the packet to the correct output port. This happens in nanoseconds using dedicated hardware (the switching fabric) and must handle millions of packets per second. Routing is a global, control-plane operation: routing protocols (like OSPF or BGP) exchange topology information with other routers across the network, run path-computation algorithms, and build the forwarding tables that the data plane uses. This happens over seconds or minutes on the router's CPU.
answer.what_breaks: If forwarding decisions were made by running the full routing algorithm on every arriving packet, the router's CPU — which operates in millisecond timescales — could not keep up with packet arrival rates (nanosecond timescales). Line-rate packet processing would be impossible. This is why modern routers maintain a pre-computed forwarding table (TCAM hardware for O(1) lookup) and run routing protocols asynchronously in the background. Collapsing the two would reduce a high-speed router to a software-switch processing a tiny fraction of its capacity.
answer.common_mistake: Students know both terms but describe them as varying levels of the same process ('routing is more global, forwarding is more local') without capturing that they operate on completely different timescales and are implemented in completely different hardware/software components.
answer.deeper_concept: The separation of control plane and data plane as a performance and scalability architecture; why SDN makes this separation even more explicit by physically moving the control plane off the router

questions.topic: The Hourglass Model and IP as Narrow Waist
questions.prompt: The Internet's hourglass architecture places IP as the 'narrow waist.' (A) Explain what property of IP enables the hourglass model — what specifically does placing IP in the middle allow above and below it? (B) A network researcher argues: 'The hourglass is a historical accident, not an optimal design. A different narrow waist would have been better.' Is this a reasonable argument? What is the cost of changing the narrow waist today?

parts.A: IP provides a minimal, uniform abstraction: it delivers datagrams on a best-effort basis with a standard address format (32-bit IPv4 or 128-bit IPv6). By requiring only that a link layer can carry IP datagrams and that an application can use IP's datagram service, the hourglass allows any physical technology (Ethernet, WiFi, fiber, copper, satellite — running over anything) to interoperate with any application (HTTP, streaming, gaming, VoIP) without either layer needing to know about the specifics of the other. IP acts as a translation layer: applications do not care whether the physical medium is fiber or WiFi, and physical networks do not care whether the application is email or video.
parts.B: The researcher's argument is entirely reasonable as a technical matter. IP was designed in the 1970s for a much smaller, more homogeneous network. Modern requirements (mobility, QoS, multicast, security) are awkward fits for the original IP design, and many of IPv4's limitations (address exhaustion, lack of encryption, fragmentation complexity) stem from early design choices. However, the practical cost of replacing IP as the narrow waist is prohibitive: every router, every OS network stack, every application, and every piece of network hardware worldwide implements IP. The switching cost is not technical — it is the massive installed base (sometimes called 'ossification'). IPv6, which is a relatively modest evolution of IPv4, took decades to achieve partial deployment. A wholesale replacement of IP would require coordinated changes at billions of devices with no clear transition path.

answer.common_mistake: Students treat the hourglass as an intentional, optimal design rather than an emergent property of IP's early success and subsequent lock-in. The distinction between 'what the hourglass enables' (technical flexibility) and 'why it persists' (installed base) is frequently missed.
answer.deeper_concept: Network ossification: how successful standards become permanent fixtures regardless of technical merit; the difference between optimal design and deployable design in large-scale infrastructure

sections.title: Scenario Analysis and Protocol Behavior
sections.instructions: Each scenario presents a realistic networking situation. Analyze the behavior step by step and address every part of the question.

questions.topic: Statistical Multiplexing — Probability and Capacity Planning

scenario.setup: An ISP has a shared 10 Mbps uplink. Each of its residential customers requires exactly 1 Mbps when active, and each customer is independently active 8% of the time.

parts.label: A
parts.prompt: How many customers can the ISP support with circuit switching?

parts.label: B
parts.prompt: With packet switching, the ISP wants the probability of more than 10 customers being simultaneously active to be below 1%. Using the binomial probability formula, set up (but do not fully evaluate) the expression for the congestion probability with 80 customers. Explain qualitatively whether 80 customers is likely to satisfy the 1% threshold.

parts.label: C
parts.prompt: The ISP doubles its residential base to 160 customers on the same 10 Mbps link. A customer complains that their internet feels slower even though no one seems to be using the internet at the same time. Give two distinct technical explanations for why this can happen even under statistical multiplexing.

A.answer: With circuit switching, each customer requires a permanently reserved 1 Mbps slice. Total capacity = 10 Mbps. Maximum customers = 10 / 1 = 10 customers.
A.note: The 11th customer would be blocked — they would receive a busy signal or connection refused.

B.qualitative_assessment: With 80 customers each active 8% of the time, the expected number active at any moment is 80 × 0.08 = 6.4 customers — well below the 10-customer capacity. The binomial distribution with n=80, p=0.08 has a mean of 6.4 and standard deviation ≈ √(80 × 0.08 × 0.92) ≈ 2.43. The threshold of 10 is approximately (10 - 6.4) / 2.43 ≈ 1.48 standard deviations above the mean. By the normal approximation, P(X > 10) ≈ P(Z > 1.48) ≈ 7% — this likely exceeds the 1% threshold. The ISP would need to reduce customer count or accept higher congestion probability. (A value of ~120-130 customers typically brings this below 1% for these parameters.)
B.note: The setup expression is what matters for exam credit; full evaluation requires a calculator.

C.explanation_1: Queuing delay is non-linear and increases sharply as utilization approaches capacity. Even at 50% average utilization, brief bursts of simultaneous activity create queues whose delay is much higher than average — the customer experiences these peaks as 'slowness' even though they don't observe other simultaneous users.
C.explanation_2: Propagation and transmission delays are always present regardless of load. A customer may be using the link exclusively but experiencing high latency to a distant server (propagation delay dominated by physical distance) or downloading a large file that fills the 10 Mbps pipe entirely — giving them 1 Mbps throughput even with no competition, because the bottleneck is their own share of the shared link at that instant.

answer.common_mistake: For Part B, students set up P(X = 10) instead of P(X > 10). Congestion occurs when demand exceeds capacity — the threshold is strictly greater than, not equal to. For Part C, students give only the 'other users are active but you can't see them' explanation and miss the fundamentally different explanation of queuing delay non-linearity.
answer.deeper_concept: The gap between average-case and worst-case performance under statistical multiplexing; why link utilization percentages are misleading without accounting for delay variance

questions.topic: FDM vs. TDM — Resource Division Mechanics

scenario.setup: A circuit-switched network has a 12 Mbps link shared among 4 users.

parts.label: A
parts.prompt: Under FDM, what transmission rate does each user get? If User 3 is idle, what happens to their allocated bandwidth?

parts.label: B
parts.prompt: Under TDM with 4 slots per frame and a frame rate of 8000 frames/second, what is the transmission rate per user? How does User 3's silence affect the other users?

parts.label: C
parts.prompt: A fifth user suddenly wants to join. What happens under FDM? What happens under TDM?

A.rate_per_user: 12 Mbps / 4 = 3 Mbps per user (each gets a dedicated 3 Mbps frequency band)
A.idle_impact: User 3's 3 Mbps frequency band sits idle — no other user can use it. The total effective throughput of the link drops from 12 Mbps to 9 Mbps while User 3 is silent, even though 3 Mbps of capacity is physically available. This is the core inefficiency of static resource allocation.

B.slot_size_bits: Total link rate = 12 Mbps. With 4 slots per frame and 8000 frames/second, each slot carries 12,000,000 / (4 × 8000) = 12,000,000 / 32,000 = 375 bits per slot. Each user transmits at 8000 slots/second × 375 bits = 3,000,000 bps = 3 Mbps.
B.idle_impact: User 3's time slots are wasted — they transmit silence (or padding). The other three users still only transmit in their own slots; they cannot use User 3's slots even though they are empty. Same static allocation problem as FDM, just in the time domain instead of frequency domain.

C.FDM: Under FDM, the fifth user cannot join unless the frequency spectrum is repartitioned. Each existing user's band must be narrowed from 3 Mbps to 2.4 Mbps (12/5), requiring reconfiguration of all five endpoints' radio hardware. In practice, this requires a new 'call setup' that renegotiates the frequency allocation — this is exactly the 'call blocking' problem: if the system is designed for exactly 4 users, the 5th user receives a busy signal.
C.TDM: Under TDM, adding a fifth user requires adding a 5th slot to each frame. This either increases the frame size (reducing the bit rate per slot if frame rate stays constant) or increases the frame rate (which may not be physically possible). Again, the connection is typically blocked if the system's fixed frame structure cannot accommodate more slots.

answer.common_mistake: Students confuse FDM and TDM by saying FDM is 'better' because it's continuous. Both have identical static allocation problems — the difference is only in the dimension being divided (frequency vs. time). Both waste resources when users are idle.
answer.deeper_concept: Static resource reservation as the fundamental inefficiency of circuit switching; why both FDM and TDM fail to exploit idle capacity and how this motivates statistical multiplexing

sections.title: Troubleshooting and Failure Scenarios
sections.instructions: Diagnose the problem at the protocol or architecture level. Identify the root cause and explain the correct behavior.

questions.topic: Delay Pathology: Low Utilization but High Latency
questions.scenario: A network operations engineer measures the following on a 100 Mbps router output link: average traffic load is 20 Mbps (20% utilization), but users are reporting latency spikes of 300–500 ms every few seconds. The engineer concludes: 'At only 20% utilization, there should be no queuing delay at all — this must be a routing protocol problem.' Identify the flaw in the engineer's reasoning and give two distinct technical explanations for why high queuing delay can occur at 20% average utilization.

answer.flaw: The engineer is applying average utilization to a conclusion that requires instantaneous behavior analysis. Queuing delay is not determined by average utilization — it is determined by whether the instantaneous arrival rate exceeds the service rate at any given moment. A 20% average utilization is perfectly consistent with frequent short bursts that saturate the link temporarily.

explanation_1.detail: Network traffic is notoriously bursty — arrivals cluster in time due to synchronized application behavior (e.g., many TCP connections entering slow-start simultaneously, scheduled batch transfers, DNS response cascades). A 20% average utilization can mask a traffic pattern where the link is idle 80% of the time and 100% saturated for bursts of 20-50 ms. During saturation bursts, packets queue and experience 300+ ms delays before the burst clears. The averaging hides this.

explanation_2.detail: Modern router buffers are often over-provisioned (hundreds of milliseconds of buffering capacity at the link rate). When a burst arrives, instead of dropping packets quickly and allowing TCP to back off, the router fills its large buffer. Even as the burst subsides and average utilization drops, the already-queued packets must drain through the buffer one by one at 100 Mbps — introducing hundreds of milliseconds of queuing delay for all subsequent packets that arrive while the buffer drains. This is known as 'bufferbloat.'

answer.common_mistake: Students accept the engineer's conclusion at face value, not recognizing that average statistics routinely hide pathological instantaneous behavior. Queuing theory (specifically the M/M/1 queue model) shows that delay increases non-linearly as utilization approaches 100% — and bursts temporarily push utilization to 100% regardless of the average.
answer.deeper_concept: The difference between average and instantaneous utilization in network performance analysis; bufferbloat as a real-world pathology caused by excessive buffering; why active queue management (RED/CoDel) exists

questions.scenario: A developer is sending 1-byte sensor readings over a WiFi network using TCP/IP. Each reading is sent as a separate TCP segment. They notice that even though sensor data is only 1 byte, Wireshark shows each transmission consuming roughly 66+ bytes on the wire. They conclude: 'The network stack is malfunctioning — it is adding 65 bytes of garbage to my 1-byte messages.' Correct the developer's understanding and explain what those 65+ bytes are, layer by layer.

answer.correction: The network stack is functioning exactly as designed. The 65+ bytes are protocol headers added by each layer of the stack through encapsulation — they are not garbage but necessary control information that enables addressing, routing, reliable delivery, and error detection.

breakdown.TCP_header: 20 bytes minimum — contains source and destination port numbers, sequence number, acknowledgment number, flags (SYN/ACK/FIN), window size for flow control, and checksum. Required for process-to-process multiplexing and TCP's reliability mechanism.
breakdown.IP_header: 20 bytes minimum — contains source and destination IP addresses (for routing), TTL, protocol field, and header checksum. Required for network-layer routing from source host to destination host.
breakdown.Ethernet_frame_header_and_trailer: 26 bytes — 14-byte header (destination MAC 6B, source MAC 6B, EtherType 2B) plus 4-byte CRC trailer. Required for link-layer addressing and error detection on the local segment.
breakdown.total_overhead: 20 + 20 + 26 = 66 bytes of headers/trailer for 1 byte of payload. Overhead ratio: 66/67 = 98.5% overhead.

answer.lesson: This extreme overhead ratio is a direct argument against sending many tiny messages. The developer should batch sensor readings (e.g., send 100 readings per packet) to reduce the per-byte overhead from 66x to near 1x. This is a fundamental application design principle: match message granularity to the protocol overhead cost.
answer.common_mistake: Students know encapsulation as a concept but cannot enumerate the actual headers and their sizes. They also often forget the Ethernet trailer (CRC) when counting overhead.
answer.deeper_concept: Protocol overhead as a real cost that shapes application design; why small-message protocols like MQTT and CoAP exist for IoT; the practical consequence of the 'Matryoshka doll' encapsulation model

sections.title: Edge Cases and Synthesis
sections.instructions: These questions require integrating multiple concepts or reasoning about subtle edge cases. Read carefully.

questions.topic: Store-and-Forward: When Does It Hurt the Most?
questions.prompt:
Two networks are being compared. Network A uses store-and-forward packet switching with 10-hop paths and 1 Mbps links. Network B uses cut-through switching, where a router begins forwarding a packet as soon as the header is received (64 bytes), rather than waiting for the entire packet. Both networks carry 1500-byte packets. Ignore propagation delay.

(A) Calculate the end-to-end transmission delay in Network A for one packet across 10 hops.
(B) Estimate the saving that cut-through switching provides per hop in Network B, and the total saving across 10 hops.
(C) Under what traffic condition does the cut-through advantage nearly disappear?

B.cut_through_per_hop: Router starts forwarding after receiving 64 bytes (header). Per-hop delay in cut-through ≈ 64 bytes × 8 / 1,000,000 = 512 bits / 1 Mbps = 0.000512 s = 0.512 ms per hop (the latency to receive just the header).
B.saving_per_hop: 12 ms - 0.512 ms ≈ 11.5 ms saved per hop
B.total_saving: However, the last hop must fully transmit the packet regardless (the destination receives all bits). So the saving applies at N-1 = 9 intermediate hops. Total saving ≈ 9 × 11.5 ms ≈ 103.5 ms. The cut-through network delivers the packet in roughly 0.512 × 10 + (1500-64) bytes × 8 / 1Mbps ≈ 5.12 ms + 11.5 ms ≈ 16.6 ms vs. 120 ms — a roughly 7× reduction in forwarding delay.
B.note: Exact cut-through delay calculation: first bit arrives at destination after header-arrival-delay × 9 intermediate hops + full packet reception at final hop. The dominant term is actually the propagation delay in real networks.

C.answer: The cut-through advantage nearly disappears when the router encounters a speed mismatch — the incoming link is faster than the outgoing link. In that case, cut-through cannot forward the packet faster than the outgoing link can absorb it, so the router is forced to buffer the full packet anyway (effectively reverting to store-and-forward behavior). Cut-through also cannot help when error-checking is needed: if the packet has a CRC error detected at the end, a cut-through router has already forwarded a corrupt packet downstream — store-and-forward catches this before forwarding. In error-prone environments, cut-through propagates errors through the network.

answer.common_mistake: Students forget that cut-through cannot save delay on the final hop — the destination must receive all bits before passing the packet up the stack. They also miss the error-propagation problem with cut-through in noisy environments.
answer.deeper_concept: Store-and-forward vs. cut-through as a latency vs. error-propagation tradeoff; why modern high-speed data center switches often use cut-through for latency-sensitive workloads

questions.topic: End-to-End Principle: Where Should Reliability Live?
questions.prompt:
The Internet's 'smart edges, dumb core' design pushes reliability (retransmission, ordering, error recovery) to the end systems rather than building it into the network core routers.

(A) Give two concrete technical benefits of this design for the network core.
(B) A critic argues: 'This is wasteful — if a packet is corrupted on hop 3 of a 10-hop path, the end-to-end design forces the sender to retransmit the entire path, wasting bandwidth on hops 1–2 and 4–10 that worked fine.' Is this a valid criticism? How would a hop-by-hop reliability design address this, and what would it cost?
(C) UDP deliberately provides no reliability — it is connectionless and unordered. Why would any application voluntarily use an unreliable transport protocol? Give two distinct real-world application categories and explain why each prefers UDP over TCP.

A.benefit_1: Routers remain simple and fast. A router that does not track connection state, sequence numbers, or retransmission timers can forward packets using only a forwarding table lookup — achievable in hardware at nanosecond speeds. Adding per-flow reliability to the core would require routers to maintain state for every active connection simultaneously, ballooning memory requirements and slowing per-packet processing.
A.benefit_2: Failure isolation and protocol flexibility. When reliability is in the endpoints, a router failure simply causes packet loss — the end systems detect this and retransmit. No mid-network connection state is lost or corrupted. Applications that do not need reliability (e.g., DNS, video streaming) can simply choose not to use it, rather than paying the overhead of network-layer reliability whether they want it or not.

B.validity: The criticism is technically valid as a narrow efficiency argument. Hop-by-hop reliability (like in older X.25 networks) would detect and retransmit a corrupted packet at hop 3 immediately, without involving the original sender and without wasting bandwidth on the clean hops.
B.cost: Hop-by-hop reliability requires every router to: (1) maintain a receive buffer and sequence number state per connection, (2) send acknowledgments back to the previous hop, (3) implement retransmission timers, and (4) coordinate state across all hops for any given connection. This reintroduces exactly the complexity that the end-to-end principle removed from routers. In practice, modern fiber links have extremely low bit-error rates (< 10^-12), so corruption is rare — the marginal benefit of hop-by-hop repair is tiny and does not justify the massive overhead of stateful routers.

category_1.application: Real-time audio/video (VoIP, video conferencing, live streaming)
category_1.reason: A retransmitted packet arrives too late to be played — the audio sample it contained is already past its playback deadline. TCP's retransmission mechanism would deliver an old packet that is now useless while delaying all subsequent packets in the stream (head-of-line blocking). UDP allows the application to simply skip the lost frame and continue with the current timestamp. A small glitch is preferable to a large stall.

category_2.application: DNS, DHCP, and other short request-response protocols
category_2.reason: These protocols send tiny single-packet queries and expect single-packet replies. TCP's three-way handshake setup and teardown would triple the number of round trips required for a transaction that consists of one request and one reply. UDP's connectionless nature allows a query to be sent immediately. If no reply arrives, the application simply retransmits — implementing its own trivial reliability that is far simpler than TCP's full machinery for such short exchanges.

answer.common_mistake: For Part C, students say 'UDP is used when speed matters' without explaining why TCP is specifically harmful for those applications. The head-of-line blocking problem (TCP holds newer data back behind a retransmit) is the specific mechanism that makes TCP worse than UDP for real-time media.
answer.deeper_concept: The end-to-end argument as a foundational Internet design principle; the tension between network simplicity and application diversity; why UDP's 'unreliability' is a feature, not a bug, for the right applications

post_exam_critique.rationale: Q6 requires isolating all four delay components in a scenario with deliberate misdirection (incoming link rate vs. outgoing link rate). Q7 demands the pipelining formula from first principles across all four sub-parts, including a qualitative asymptotic argument in Part D. Q12 integrates binomial probability, capacity planning intuition, and queuing non-linearity in one scenario. Q14 attacks the most common operational misconception in networking — equating average utilization with performance — and requires two mechanistically distinct explanations. Q17 is a multi-part synthesis requiring the student to simultaneously defend, critique, and apply the end-to-end principle.

questions_to_strengthen.note: The circuit switching idle-resource T/F is conceptually important but is close to a direct definition lookup. A stronger version would present a TDM network with a specific number of slots and ask: 'User 2 has been idle for 5 minutes. User 5 arrives and wants to join. Walk through what happens' — forcing the student to apply idle-waste and call-blocking in a concrete scenario.

FDM/TDM calculation is solid but Part C (fifth user joining) is somewhat predictable. A harder variant: 'Under TDM, three of the four users agree to share their slots with the idle fourth user. Design the modified frame structure and calculate the throughput each active user now receives.' This tests whether students understand that TDM slot reassignment requires renegotiation and coordination.

under_tested_concepts: Traceroute mechanics at the intro level: how TTL-expiry produces per-hop RTT measurements and why three probes are sent
The caravan analogy in quantitative form: a scenario where propagation speed is high but transmission rate is low and asking which packets are 'in flight' at a given moment
IoT and NextG teleoperation as motivating examples: why C&C uses downlink and sensor data uses uplink, and the asymmetric bandwidth implications
The difference between Internet Standards (RFCs, IETF) and de facto standards: why the Internet protocol stack is defined by rough consensus and running code, not ISO bodies
Message segmentation overhead quantified: for a fixed total message size, at what packet size does header overhead exceed pipelining gain — the optimal packet size tradeoff
Multicast addressing: what problem it solves that unicast cannot solve efficiently and that broadcast wastes bandwidth on
